misconceptionSummary: Students add decimal places correctly but miscount, treating them from the left rather than the right of the answer.

visuals.ruleText: Add the decimal places in both factors — the answer has that many

steps.narration: When multiplying two decimals, add together the number of decimal places in each factor. Two point three has one decimal place, one point four has one decimal place — so the answer has two decimal places in total.

Ignore the decimal points and multiply twenty-three by fourteen. Twenty-three times fourteen: twenty-three times four is ninety-two, twenty-three times ten is two hundred and thirty, total three hundred and twenty-two. The answer needs two decimal places.

Place the decimal two places from the right of three hundred and twenty-two: three point two two. Estimate to check: two times one is two, so the answer should be close to two or three — three point two two is reasonable.
Three point two two. Add the decimal places in both factors to find the total, multiply as whole numbers, then count that many places from the right in the answer.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 2.3 × 1.4 = 32.2 (placed decimal 1 place instead of 2). Add decimal places: 1 + 1 = 2. Place 2 from the right: 322 → 3.22.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Add the decimal places from both factors — not just one of them. Two point three and one point four each have one decimal place, so the answer has two: 322 → 3.22.
